title: Market Bubbles
theory: A market bubble occurs when the price of an asset significantly exceeds its intrinsic value, driven by exuberant market behavior. Bubbles typically follow a pattern of stealth, awareness, mania, and blow-off phases.
example: During the late 1990s, the Dot-Com bubble saw internet-based companies reach astronomical valuations despite having little to no revenue.

caseStudy.title: The Tulip Mania of 1637
caseStudy.text: In the Dutch Golden Age, contract prices for some bulbs of the recently introduced and fashionable tulip reached extraordinarily high levels and then dramatically collapsed.

quiz.question: Which phase of a bubble is characterized by widespread public enthusiasm and irrational exuberance?

title: Diversification
theory: Diversification is a risk management strategy that mixes a wide variety of investments within a portfolio. The rationale behind this technique is that a portfolio constructed of different kinds of assets will, on average, yield higher long-term returns and lower the risk of any individual holding or security.
example: Instead of investing all your money in a single technology stock, you spread it across technology, healthcare, energy, and consumer goods.

caseStudy.title: The Lost Decade
caseStudy.text: Investors who were heavily concentrated in US large-cap tech stocks during the 2000s saw negative returns for a decade, while those diversified into emerging markets and bonds saw positive growth.

quiz.question: What is the primary goal of diversification?
options: To reduce exposure to any single asset's risk
